title: Three Rounds of OPC Review to Polish a Preview Dashboard
summary: Building ink-flow's preview dashboard with apple-style-ui — frosted glass, dark theme, channel sidebar, live preview iframe. Three rounds of OPC review took it from P0 bugs to pixel-perfect. A practical account of using agent review to elevate UI quality.
body:
## The Need

ink-flow needed a preview dashboard. Before publishing, I wanted to see what content looked like on each platform — WeChat's 375px mobile view, Zhihu's 690px desktop view, Twitter thread's segmented layout.

The previous approach was opening HTML files manually. With 6 platforms, opening 6 files every time was painful.

So I needed a dashboard: left sidebar listing all channels, click to switch, right side with live iframe preview.

## apple-style-ui Skill Driving Design

I used the apple-style-ui skill to define the visual style:

```
/apple-style-ui Build an ink-flow preview dashboard with Apple aesthetic.
Layout: left sidebar with channel list, right side split into top
(materials/files area) and bottom (live preview iframe).
```

The skill provides a complete design token system:
- Surface: `rgba() + blur(40px) saturate(180%)`
- Border: `rgba(0,0,0,0.08-0.18)` — never hard borders
- Radius: sidebar 16px, dock 20px
- Font: `-apple-system, 'SF Pro Text'`
- Motion: `cubic-bezier(0.34,1.2,0.64,1)` spring curve

preview.py is a single-file Python HTTP server — all HTML/CSS/JS inline in Python strings. Sounds hacky, but for a dev tool: single file = zero dependencies = zero configuration.

## Round 1: Core Functionality

The first version implemented:
- Sidebar showing All Materials + platform channels
- All Materials view: file card grid with filename, size, type
- Platform view: iframe loading `/api/preview/{platform}`
- Dark theme + frosted glass sidebar

OPC review (frontend + designer roles) found issues:

**P0 (must fix):**
- Cover images not showing in All Materials view
- WeChat preview was full-width, should be limited to 375px

**P1 (should fix):**
- a11y: nav items missing `role="button"` and `tabindex="0"`
- Status badges lacking semantic colors (ready = green, published = blue)
- Topbar not updating title on channel switch

## Round 2: Fix P0 + Most P1

Fixed cover display, iframe width limits, a11y attributes. Also hardened the channel metadata endpoint (`/api/platforms`) — no `basedir` path leaking.

Second review found new issues:
- Frosted glass blur flickering on some channel switches
- File card hover effect missing spring animation
- Scrollbar too visible in dark theme

## Round 3: Polish

Pure visual polish:
- `scrollbar-width: none` to hide scrollbars
- `.material-card:hover` with spring transition (`transform: translateY(-2px)`)
- Sidebar `.nav-item.active` with subtle glow
- `<html lang="zh">` for a11y compliance

After three rounds, the dashboard matched macOS Sonoma's design language: dark background, frosted glass sidebar, solid content areas for readability, spring curves on all interactive elements.

## Test Coverage

This dashboard isn't just "looks good" — it has 36 automated tests:

**API layer (21 tests):**
- `GET /` returns dashboard HTML with slug and platform data
- `GET /api/platforms` returns JSON without server path leaks
- `GET /api/files` returns file list with size and ext
- `GET /api/file/{name}` returns content, path traversal blocked
- `GET /api/image/{path}` returns PNG with cache headers
- `GET /api/preview/{platform}` returns rendered HTML
- Unknown routes correctly return 404

**Browser UI layer (15 tests, Playwright headless Chromium):**
- Sidebar visible, channel count ≥ 3
- All Materials selected by default, showing file cards
- Click WeChat channel → iframe loads → topbar updates
- Click Zhihu channel → switch succeeds
- Return to All Materials → view restores
- Dark background verification (low RGB values)
- Sidebar backdrop-filter blur verification
- Scrollbar hidden verification
- Keyboard navigation (tabindex) verification
- Full-page screenshot artifact

Tests use a module-scoped fixture starting the server (non-default port 19877 to avoid conflicts), all API and UI tests sharing one server instance. The 36 tests complete in seconds.

## Takeaway

The value of three OPC review rounds isn't bug-finding (though it did find bugs) — it's that **it forces you to examine UI from different role perspectives.** The frontend role cares about implementation quality; the designer role cares about visual consistency. Reviewing alone, it's hard to cover both dimensions simultaneously.

Another insight: **for dev tools like preview servers, single-file beats any framework.** Zero dependencies means it runs anywhere. `python3 preview.py /path/to/crosspost` and you're up. No npm install, no vite config.
